threshold of 0.1 gram in order to align the thresholds with the current TRI thresholds in 
order to minimize complexity and burden. The thresholds for certain TAC without a URE 
or RfC where a reporting threshold could not be determined as described above, are 
based on the APEN thresholds of 250 lbs/year. In the context of TAC reporting 
however, the threshold of 250 lbs/year is based on the controlled actual emissions 
across the entire TAC source instead of the uncontrolled emission-point level thresholds 
that exist in the APEN program. The reporting thresholds in Appendix C were adopted 
to reduce reporting burden and obtain useful TAC data. They are not being adopted as 
health-based standards. Health-based standards for PTACs will be the subject of future 
rulemaking and are subject to General Assembly approval. The reporting thresholds are 
also not being adopted as permitting thresholds. Reporting emissions at or above the de 
minimis thresholds does not indicate the need for a facility or stationary source to 
assess risk to receptors. In setting these reporting thresholds, the Commission intends 
for reporting to inform the Division’s TAC program in the future, and is not concluding or 
suggesting that reporting emissions above the de minimis threshold indicates a potential 
health risk to individuals or potential receptors near the source. In keeping with this 
approach, the Commission understands that the Division will not use reporting above 
the de minimis thresholds alone to presume any potential for risk from facility or source 
and will take steps as necessary to educate the public on understanding potential for 
risk from exposures to air toxics consistent with the commission’s intent in setting de 
minimis thresholds. 
The Commission requests that the Division evaluate whether revisions to this reporting 
rule are warranted in the future, and in doing so, consider alternative methodologies or 
alternative values for de minimis reporting thresholds to further streamline reporting 
while still achieving the desired information to inform future iterations of the Division’s air 
toxics program. 
Other Items 
While these reporting revisions reflect the vast majority of recommendations in the gap 
analysis report, there are some recommendations that the Commission did not adopt in 
these rule revisions. Regarding the recommendations that related to potential changes 
to the APEN program, we anticipate that future revisions may be needed to further

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
557 
streamline the reporting and future use of actual emission data submitted on APEN 
forms. However, given that APEN are the current sole basis of annual emission fees, 
those changes cannot be made at this time. Additionally, regarding the recommendation 
to potentially retain TAC reporting requirements on sources who may change from 
major or synthetic minors to minor sources, there is insufficient data available to support 
the need for this reporting and they could be considered at a future iteration. 
Fees 
The Commission adopted revisions to Regulation Number 3, Section VI.D. to increase 
emissions fees and permit processing fees to cover the indirect and direct costs 
required to develop and administer the programs; established pursuant to this article 7. 
The Commission occasionally updates fees to adjust for inflation or account for new 
statutory requirements. These fees fund Division programs and services to improve and 
protect air quality in Colorado and enhanced state statutory and federal requirements to 
protect clean air in recent years have gone beyond what current fees assessed and 
collected support. 
The Commission adopted revisions to Regulation Number 3, Part A, Section VI.D.1. to 
increase the APEN filing fee from $242.00 to $363.00. The Commission adopted 
revisions to Regulation Number 3, Part A, Section VI.D.2. to increase the permit 
processing fees from $119.00 per hour to $180.00. The Commission adopted revisions 
to Regulation Number 3, Part A, Section VI.D.3. to increase the annual emission fees 
for regulated pollutants from $36.00 per ton to $60.00 and for hazardous air pollutants 
from $239.00 per ton to $398.00. However, for emissions in 2025, the Commission 
increased the annual emission fees for regulated pollutants from $36.00 per ton to 
$84.00 and for hazardous air pollutants from $239.00 per ton to $557.00, to be billed in 
calendar year 2026. Beginning with 2026 emissions, the fees will revert back to the 
$60/ton and $239/ton figures. 
Recognizing the significant fees imposed on regulated entities and the anticipated 
additional fee increases in future years, the Commission requests the Division to 
annually evaluate the Division’s direct and indirect program costs, provide the 
evaluation to the Commission, and request a rulemaking hearing to adjust the costs if 
program costs are determined during that assessment to be higher, lower, or 
alternatively funded. The Commission requests the Division, in consultation with 
emission sources and other stakeholders, assess the fee program and make 
recommendations to evaluate the need to modify the fee structure, particularly related to 
TAC emissions; address the impact of impending closures of large emitting sources on 
current fee payors; reduce costs through efficiencies and alternative funding sources; 
and identify needed legislative and regulatory changes. The Commission expects the 
Division to continue engagement with fee payers and other stakeholders to explore

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
558 
broad-based options to modernize the issuance of air quality permits and improve 
transparency while protecting public health and complying with legal requirements 
through continued implementation of the stationary source control fund (SSCF) 
meetings. The Commission expects the SSCF meetings to evaluate and the Division to 
implement efficiency improvements including, but not limited to, reducing permit and 
modeling processing timelines; streamlining the processing of administrative and minor 
permit modifications; increasing transparency in the Division’s permit processing, billing, 
emissions inventories, and compliance inspections; and ensuring the Division’s forms, 
reporting tools, and electronic interfaces are user-friendly, efficient, and functional for 
the regulated community. As part of these discussions, the Commission requests the 
Division work with stakeholders to identify additional opportunities from providing 
transparency on process improvements now and throughout the currently ongoing 
modernization effort. Further, the Commission requests an annual briefing be provided 
to the Commission (in addition to updates in the Division Director’s report) and routine 
updates be provided to interested stakeholders and to interested stakeholders through 
SSCF meetings, or other public meetings as appropriate, describing what process 
improvements have been implemented and providing information on process 
performance including updates on permit processing times, permit backlogs, process 
modernization efforts, and issued guidance. The Commission intends to promulgate any 
future fee increases with sufficient lead time to allow fee payers to plan their operations 
and budget for the additional costs. To ensure equity, the Commission also intends to 
assess emissions fees at the same rate (i.e., dollars per ton) for all emissions that occur 
during each calendar year. Accordingly, the Commission expects the Division to submit 
any future fee rulemaking petitions early enough to apply the revised fees at the 
beginning of the next calendar year. 
The Commission also recognizes that sustained emissions reductions from stationary 
sources have reduced the number of tons of criteria pollutants, hazardous air pollutants, 
and greenhouse gases upon which fees are assessed under Section VI.D. The number 
of tons subject to Section VI.D. fees are expected to continue declining, which will 
further reduce the base upon which fees are paid. Accordingly, the Commission 
requests the Division to evaluate options for revising the fee structure to establish a 
more stable and broad-based funding mechanism. The Division’s evaluation should 
include identifying opportunities for assessing fees on persons or entities other than 
stationary sources. 
Further, these revisions address typographical cross-reference errors found within the 
regulation. 
Federal vs. State-Only Conditions (if applicable)

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
559 
There are no direct federal requirements that correspond with the new rule. However, to 
the extent the new rule could be construed to exceed or differ from any related 
requirements of federal law, the Commission provides the following additional 
statement, consistent with section 25-7-110.5(5)(a), C.R.S.: 
(I) Any federal requirements that are applicable to this situation with a commentary on 
those requirements; 
There are several federal regulations that are related, but not directly applicable, to 
Colorado’s adoption of annual TAC reporting regulations. These federal reporting 
programs are not directly applicable, as the Commission’s consideration of a state 
annual TAC reporting regulation is a state statutory requirement under section 25-7-
109.5(4)(c)(III) C.R.S. The related federal reporting requirements are: 
a. 
U.S. EPA Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) 
The EPA TRI collects annual reports from facilities that discharge chemicals into the 
environment via the air, water, land, or underground injection, and any associated 
pollution prevention strategies. Sources that must report to the TRI are based on 
chemical activity thresholds at a facility, are covered industries based on certain NAICS 
codes, or report based on the Emergency Planning and Community Right to Know Act 
(EPCRA) section 313(b)(2). Each chemical listed under the TRI has specific activity 
thresholds that may trigger reporting. 
The Commission adopted a requirement for sources that report to TRI to also report 
annual emissions of any TRI pollutant above the de minimis reporting thresholds in 
Appendix C to the Division. This requirement allows the Division to evaluate emissions 
data currently reported by minor sources solely to TRI with the annual TAC reports and 
to evaluate instances where emissions reported to the TRI are vastly different than the 
emissions reported to the Division, improving TAC modeling efforts and programs. 
b. 
U.S. EPA Air Emissions Reporting Rule (AERR) 
The EPA collects toxic air emissions data through the AERR. At present, the AERR 
gathers data from states on CAP: sulfur dioxide SO2, volatile organic compounds 
(VOC), nitrogen oxides (NOx), primary particulate matter 10 (PM10) and 2.5 (PM2.5)), 
carbon monoxide (CO), lead (Pb) and ammonia (NH3). State reporting of federal HAP 
to the EPA is currently voluntary, although the Division has provided HAP voluntarily to 
EPA for many decades based on data reported under the APEN program. States are 
required to report CAP data for certain larger sources based on potential to emit. Under 
the current AERR, states report annually for ‘very large’ sources and triennially for 
‘large’ sources The thresholds for ‘large’ and ‘very large’ sources are dependent upon 
non-attainment area (NAA) classification for one or more National Ambient Air Quality

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
560 
Standards (NAAQS). The Commission’s adopted revisions at Part A, Section IX., are 
structured in a way that is largely compatible with the current AERR. 
The Commission adopted revisions at Part B, Section IX. require annual reporting of 
emissions from all sources required to obtain an operating permit, which is more 
frequent than the triennial requirement in the current AERR, but consistent with the 
annual requirement in section 25-7-109.5(4)(c)(III) C.R.S. 
(II) Whether the applicable federal requirements are performance-based or technology-
based and whether there is any flexibility in those requirements, and if not, why not; 
The federal requirements related to reporting of TAC or CAP emissions are not 
performance-based or technology-based. 
(III) Whether the applicable federal requirements specifically address the issues that are 
of concern to Colorado and whether data or information that would reasonably reflect 
Colorado's concern and situation was considered in the federal process that established 
the federal requirements; 
The federal reporting requirements in the EPA TRI or AERR do not specifically address 
Colorado’s statutory directive to consider a TAC reporting program that ensures annual 
reports on TAC are submitted to the Division. 
(IV) Whether the proposed requirement will improve the ability of the regulated 
community to comply in a more cost-effective way by clarifying confusing or potentially 
conflicting requirements (within or cross-media), increasing certainty, or preventing or 
reducing the need for costly retrofit to meet more stringent requirements later; 
The adopted revisions at Part A, Section IX. establish additional reporting requirements 
on the regulated community. Multiple commenters urged the Division to minimize 
potential duplicative reporting burden on impacted sources. To accomplish this 
objective, where applicable, the revisions align reporting format and procedures with 
federal requirements, to simplify and clarify requirements for owners and operators 
reporting to both federal and state programs. The Division is planning to use an EPA 
web-based reporting system, Consolidated Air Emissions Reporting System (CAERS), 
that will be pre-populated with equipment parameters and can be connected to other 
reporting programs such as the EPA TRI. Additionally, the adopted revisions for the 
annual reporting of CAP and incidental pollutants, as well as the specified small entity 
requirements, allow for flexibilities helping the Division move towards more streamlined 
reporting requirements and reducing burden on reports. Additionally, the de minimis 
reporting thresholds in Part A, Appendix C, which were not part of the initial reporting 
requirement codified at section 25-7-109.5(4)(a) C.R.S., streamlines the pollutants that 
sources must report and improves the ability of the regulated community to comply in a 
more cost-effective way.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
561 
(V) Whether there is a timing issue which might justify changing the time frame for 
implementation of federal requirements; 
These are state-specific revisions that are not implementing federal requirements. Thus, 
no major timing issue exists. However, the TRI has an annual reporting deadline of July 
1. The current AERR has a deadline for states to submit point source data by December 
31 of the year after inventory year, with grace period extension of 15 days. In Colorado, 
this submittal would include the latest data on file at the time of the submittal, which 
could reflect the inventory year but could also reflect emissions from prior years due to 
the 5-year update frequency under the APEN program. The adopted revisions establish 
a TAC reporting deadline of June 30 each year, for the previous calendar year 
emissions, which is consistent with TRI and provides time for Division staff to review 
submittals that reflect the more accurate previous calendar year emissions prior to the 
current AERR submittal deadlines. 
(VI) Whether the proposed requirement will assist in establishing and maintaining a 
reasonable margin for accommodation of uncertainty and future growth; 
The reporting revisions in Part A, Section IX. are retrospective in that they will require 
sources to report past emissions (i.e., by June 30, 2027, sources must report calendar 
year 2026 emissions data) and, therefore, are not subject to uncertainty and do not 
hinder or negatively affect future growth of facilities. 
(VII) Whether the proposed requirement establishes or maintains reasonable equity in 
the requirements for various sources; 
The reporting requirements are consistent across source types, as they are based on 
de minimis thresholds in Appendix C and, therefore, maintain reasonable equity in the 
reporting requirements. Although not all minor sources are required to report TAC 
annually, the minor sources being required to submit annual TAC reports are sources 
that emit specific pollutants of concern. The Commission believes collecting data from 
these sources is vital in its efforts to characterize and regulate TAC in the state. These 
new minor sources will also utilize the de minimis reporting thresholds in Appendix C. 
Additionally, the small entity provision at Section IX.B.3.a(iv)(A) allows qualified sources 
to report at the facility-level and maintains equity in supporting small businesses. 
(VIII) Whether others would face increased costs if a more stringent rule is not enacted; 
It is not anticipated there would be increased direct costs to others if a more stringent 
rule is not enacted. 
(IX) Whether the proposed requirement includes procedural, reporting, or monitoring 
requirements that are different from applicable federal requirements and, if so, why and

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
562 
what the “compelling reason” is for different procedural, reporting, or monitoring 
requirements; 
The adopted revisions do require additional and different reporting requirements for 
pollutants in Colorado, compared to federal reporting requirements. Reporting 
requirements beyond those required under federal law are necessary to effectively 
quantify and analyze TAC emissions in Colorado. The Commission is required by state 
statute to consider establishing an annual TAC reporting program, among other 
regulatory directives around TAC that may all be impacted by the Division’s and the 
Commission’s ability to acquire accurate and detailed TAC data from sources of air 
emissions in the state. Collecting data at the levels, frequencies, and granularity that the 
adopted revisions established will be vital to future regulations surrounding TAC, many 
of which are required by state statute. 
(X) Whether demonstrated technology is available to comply with the proposed 
requirement; 
EPA has promoted an alternative web-based data collection instrument called CAERS, 
to help collect this emissions information. This program is compatible with reporting 
under the current AERR reporting requirements. CAERS provides a unified platform for 
emissions reporting, simplifying the process for facilities that may otherwise need to 
navigate multiple reporting systems. This consolidation can lead to greater consistency 
in data reporting and potentially reduce the administrative burden on reporting entities. 
For example, this data system can also connect with a system (TRI-MEweb) to report 
TRI emissions for those sources also required to submit a TRI report. Additionally, 
CAERS provides options to submit data in a bulk upload format and provides several 
data validation checks to improve the quality of the reported data. CAERS is also able 
to collect emissions of both TAC and CAP at the emission point level and it has been 
demonstrated in use by several other state agencies. The Division is investigating the 
applicability and timing of using CAERS in the adopted annual TAC reporting program. 
(XI) Whether the proposed requirement will contribute to the prevention of pollution or 
address a potential problem and represent a more cost-effective environmental gain; 
The adopted revisions will allow for the development of a comprehensive and robust 
TAC inventory, which will enable and inform future implementation strategies to identify 
additional priority TAC, develop cost-effective emission control strategy regulations, and 
evaluate a TAC permitting program, as directed by section 25-7-109.5. C.R.S. 
(XII) Whether an alternative rule, including a no-action alternative, would address the 
required standard. 
The revisions in Part A, Section IX. achieve the statutorily mandated directive to 
consider reporting requirements that ensure annual TAC emissions are reported to the

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
563 
Division. Alternate reporting requirements could satisfy the state statutory requirements. 
For example, alternative requirements could include a variation of the proposed TAC list 
in Appendix C, different de minimis reporting thresholds, different source applicability 
criteria, or different TAC report contents. A no-action alternative would default to 
existing statutory language at section 25-7-109.5(4)(a) C.R.S., which directs annual 
TAC reporting only from sources required to obtain title V operating permits and 
synthetic minor sources. However, this alternative would omit reporting a large 
proportion of TAC emissions in the state and could prevent the Division from collecting 
important data about equipment and operations that would inform TAC regulations 
going forward. Under this alternative approach, de minimis thresholds would also not 
exist, meaning that sources could face additional reporting burdens. 
Findings of Fact 
Section 25-7-110.8, C.R.S., requirements do not apply to this rulemaking because the 
adopted rule is not directly intended to reduce air pollution. To the extent § 25-7-110.8, 
C.R.S. does apply to this rulemaking; after considering all the information in the record, 
the Commission hereby makes the determination that: 
(I) The rule is based on reasonably available, validated, reviewed, and sound scientific 
methodologies, and the Commission has considered all information submitted by 
interested parties. 
(II) The rule is not intended to address a reduction in air pollution, or, in the alternative, 
is administrative in nature. 
(III) Evidence in the record supports the finding that the rule provides benefits that justify 
the costs to government to implement the rule, and to the extent possible, minimizes 
any cost to the regulated community or public in order for the Commission to establish 
sufficiently comprehensive and robust inventories of TAC. 
(IV) The rules are the most cost-effective to achieve the necessary and desired results 
and reduction in air pollution. 
(V) The rule does not directly result in air quality benefits but addresses state statutory 
requirements in a cost-effective manner.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
564 
I.KKK. 
Adopted: November 19-21, 2025 
Revisions to Regulation Number 3: Part A, Sections I.B., II.C., and VI.E.; Part B, 
Sections III.B., III.D., and III.J.; and Part C, Sections II.E., III.C., and V.C. 
Basis 
In 2022, the Air Quality Control Commission (Commission) adopted the major stationary 
source fee provisions in Regulation Number 3, Part A, Section VI.E. The Division had 
proposed these provisions in response to the classification as a severe ozone 
nonattainment area under the 2008 ozone National Ambient Air Quality Standards 
(NAAQS) so as to include such fee program as directed by the Clean Air Act (CAA) 
section 185, should Colorado fail to attain the 2008 ozone NAAQS by the applicable 
severe attainment date. However, the provisions were based on EPA guidance that 
EPA has not updated for the 8-hour ozone NAAQS.  
The emission reduction credit (ERC) program allows for the generation of tradeable 
ERCs for voluntary emission reductions by sources beyond legal and permit 
requirements. The Commission adopted revisions to remove provisions that were based 
on outdated federal regulations and guidance, such as inapplicable terms and 
definitions, and to improve implementation.  
In House Bill 21-1266 the General Assembly determined that “state action to correct 
environmental injustice is imperative, and state policy can and should improve public 
health and the environment and improve the overall well-being of all communities… 
[and] efforts to right past wrongs and move toward environmental justice must focus on 
disproportionately impacted communities and the voices of their residents.” Therefore, 
the Commission adopted, among other things, more stringent requirements for sources 
located in or near disproportionately impacted communities, referencing § 24-4-
109(2)(b)(II), C.R.S., which includes the definition of “disproportionately impacted 
community”. Previously, the definition in this regulation referenced Version 1.0 of 
Colorado EnviroScreen as the tool used to identify disproportionately impacted 
communities, cumulatively impacted communities, and socioeconomically vulnerable 
communities in Colorado. In November 2024, the Colorado Department of Public Health 
and Environment (CDPHE) published the Colorado Disproportionately Impacted 
Community Map to be used as the primary mapping tool for identifying 
disproportionately impacted communities, cumulatively impacted communities, and 
socioeconomically vulnerable communities in Colorado. The Colorado 
Disproportionately Impacted Community Map (November 2024) uses some data from 
Colorado EnviroScreen, which was also updated from Version 1.0 to Version 2.0 in 
November 2024, but is a separate tool. When updating Colorado EnviroScreen and the 
new Colorado Disproportionately Impacted Community Map in November 2024, CDPHE 
also updated the underlying census data used to inform the tools, causing the

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
565 
classification of some communities to change. Therefore, the Commission updated the 
disproportionately impacted community definition to reference the new mapping tool and 
include the statutory criteria for mobile home communities in the definition as used in 
the regulation.  
Specific Statutory Authority 
Section 185 of the Clean Air Act (CAA) requires states with ozone nonattainment areas 
classified as Severe or Extreme to develop a fee collection rule in the event the area 
does not attain by the applicable attainment date. The Commission is tasked with 
promulgating rules necessary for the proper implementation of Colorado’s SIP. § 25-7-
105, C.R.S. The SIP must meet all requirements of the CAA and must be revised 
whenever necessary and appropriate. Id. The Commission adopted revisions to the 
CAA section 185 fee program pursuant to this authority.  
The Colorado Air Pollution Prevention and Control Act (State Air Act) authorizes the 
Commission to promulgate a comprehensive State Implementation Plan (SIP) and 
promulgate all rules that are necessary for proper implementation and administration of 
Article 7 of the State Air Act. § 25-7-105(1)(a), C.R.S. The SIP must assure attainment 
of the National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) and prevent significant 
deterioration of air quality in conformance with Article 7 of the State Air Act. § 25-7-
105(1)(a)(I), C.R.S. Moreover, section 25-7-304, C.R.S., requires the SIP provide a 
means for emission reduction offsets to be preserved for sale or use in the future if 
those reductions exceed those otherwise necessary for the granting of a permit under 
Part 3 of the State Air Act. The Commission is granted maximum flexibility in developing 
an effective air quality control program and is authorized to promulgate any such 
combination of regulations as may be necessary or desirable to carry out that program. 
§ 25-7-106(1), C.R.S. This includes, and is not limited to, requiring an owner and 
operator of a source to: (1) establish and maintain reports; (2) record or monitor 
emissions; and (3) install, use and maintain monitoring equipment. The Commission is 
tasked with promulgating regulations as necessary and proper for the orderly and 
effective administration of construction permits and renewable operating permits. § 25-
7-114.4(1), C.R.S. These regulations must implement permit and permit application 
procedures including: (1) procedures to be followed in the event of expansion or 
modification of the source; and (2) procedures to make available any permit application, 
compliance plan, or permit among other items. § 25-7-114.4(1)(i),(o), C.R.S. The 
Commission adopted revisions to the minor permit modification procedures pursuant to 
the outlined authority.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
566 
Section 24-4-109(2)(a)(I)(A), C.R.S. asserts that all statewide agencies shall use the 
definition of disproportionately impacted community as set forth in the statute. § 24-4-
109(2)(a)(I)(B), provides that a statewide agency may “prioritize or target” certain 
subsets of communities that meet the definition of disproportionately impacted 
community if the prioritization is warranted and reasonably tailored to the statewide 
action being taken. If the statewide agency making the determination to prioritize or 
target retains rulemaking authority, the agency must make this determination by rule. 
See § 24-4-109(2)(a)(I)(B). Section 24-4-109(2)(b)(IV), defines “statewide agency” to 
include any commission, thus, the Commission is authorized to prioritize or target 
certain subsets of the definition of disproportionately impacted community so long as 
that prioritization is warranted and reasonably tailored to the Commission’s action. 
Purpose 
 
Annual Actual Emissions Definition 
In 2004, the Commission adopted “annual actual emissions” to replace “actual 
emissions” throughout Part A in reference to APENs so that the source’s reported 
emissions are those actually emitted during a single year. In contrast, “actual emissions” 
was placed in Part D to be used for major stationary source determination purposes. 
Therefore, the Commission did not intend the definition of “annual actual emissions” to 
broadly impact existing requirements in Regulation Number 3. With the updated 
definition of “annual actual emissions” adopted as part of this rulemaking, the 
Commission maintains its previous stance that these changes apply only to Part A in 
reference to APENs. It is also the Commission’s understanding that this revision to the 
definition of “annual actual emissions” will not interfere with or replace the use of EPA- 
or state-specified performance tests to determine compliance.  
The Commission recognizes that emissions from malfunctions, and startups and 
shutdowns associated with malfunctions, may not be readily ascertainable.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
567 
Revisions to the emission reduction credit program 
The Commission adopted significant revisions to Part A, Section V for the ERC 
program, that included focusing and simplifying requirements, removing unnecessary 
provisions, and reorganizing sections for improved clarity. These changes improve the 
effectiveness, stringency, implementation, and administration of the program. Changes 
adopted by the Commission included revising the program so that the credits pertain to 
only Nonattainment New Source Review (NANSR) offsets for the ozone nonattainment 
area; making credits apply only to reductions of volatile organic compounds (VOC) and 
oxides of nitrogen (NOx) emissions, although future considerations may be made to 
expand the regulation to other pollutants, provided that nonattainment areas are 
designated for said pollutant(s); removing or revising definitions and references to 
provisions that are not used in Regulation Number 3, are improperly used, or are 
redundant; updating how emissions are calculated and assessed for determining offsets 
and credit applicability; revising, clarifying, and modernizing program procedures for 
more effective implementation; and replacing or removing outdated and/or inapplicable 
provisions including modeling requirements, hazardous air pollutant trading, the 
baseline emissions calculator, and interpollutant/interprecursor emissions trading. 
Owners and operators of stationary sources described in section V.D.1.a. are voluntarily 
applying for ERCs. As a condition for obtaining certified ERCs, all operating sources, 
including APEN-exempt and permit-exempt sources, are required to submit an APEN 
and apply for and accept a permit as a condition for obtaining certified ERCs, per Part 
A, Section V.D.4.f., and are subject to payment of all associated processing and filing 
fees. Shut-down sources may not be required to submit an APEN or apply for a permit. 
Under section V.D.3.f., the Division has 120 days from the receipt of an application to 
request additional information. While the Division intends to make such requests within 
this period, circumstances may arise that require the Division to request information 
after the 120-day window has elapsed. Section V.D.3.f. does not prohibit requests 
beyond 120 days; rather, the timeframe sets an expected window for when applicants 
can expect to receive inquiries about an application.  
Part A, Section V.C.1 defines the base year for the SIP planning process as the 
baseline year for reasonable further progress (RFP) demonstrations submitted to the 
EPA for the applicable NAAQS and nonattainment area. The base years (as of the 
effective date of this regulation) are 2011 for the 2008 ozone NAAQS and 2017 for the 
2015 ozone NAAQS.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
568 
Part A, Section V.D.2.a. prohibits any emission reductions from mobile sources from 
being certified as ERCs unless those sources are subject to ambient impact and new 
source review permitting. Currently, the Division’s permitting programs are not set up to 
permit mobile sources and lack a mechanism for the enforceability of these permits, and 
therefore any ERC applications for mobile sources would not be able to be processed. 
The Division is open to revisiting this rule in a future rulemaking if the Division 
establishes a process for handling these mobile source permits and would like to move 
forward with establishing a mobile source ERC program. The Commission has no 
intention of directing the development of a mobile source permitting program currently. 
A source with an emissions unit out of compliance with a nonattainment pollutant may 
submit an ERC application and may be eligible to receive ERCs even if the emission 
unit is out of compliance at time of application if the source seeks reductions beyond 
what is required for the unit to return to compliance. Reductions beyond what is required 
to return to compliance must be achieved in compliance with V.D.4.f. If upon the 
Division’s substantive review of the ERC application, the emissions unit is still out of 
compliance with the emissions limit, the Division may deny the application. 
The adopted changes help ensure that ERCs are appropriately administered for 
sources that achieve emissions offsets that ultimately reduce ozone precursors in the 
nonattainment area. 
Revisions regarding the disproportionately impacted community definitions and their use 
The Commission adopted revisions to the definitions of disproportionately impacted 
community, cumulatively impacted community, and socioeconomically vulnerable 
community (collectively referred to here as disproportionately impacted communities) to 
update the data tool used to identify such communities in Colorado and to include the 
statutory criteria for mobile home communities found in § 24-4-109(2)(b)(II)(G), C.R.S. 
The Commission directs the use of the Colorado Disproportionately Impacted 
Community Map (November 2024) to identify disproportionately impacted communities. 
The areas in Colorado that meet criteria in the definitions are displayed on the Colorado 
Disproportionately Impacted Community Map. For uses of this regulation, users must 
select the statutory criteria from § 24-4-109(2)(b)(II)(A)-(D), (F), and (G), C.R.S. Those 
criteria are labeled as: “Mobile home communities”, “Low-income population above 
40%”, “People of color population above 40%”, “Housing cost-burdened population 
above 50%”, “Linguistically isolated population above 20%”, and “Colorado 
EnviroScreen percentile score above 80”. For identifying disproportionately impacted 
communities, users select all six criteria. For identifying cumulatively impacted 
communities, users select only the criterion for “Colorado EnviroScreen percentile score 
above 80”. For identifying socioeconomically vulnerable communities, users select only 
the criteria for “Mobile home communities”, “Low-income population above 40%”,

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
569 
“People of color population above 40%”, “Housing cost-burdened population above 
50%”, and “Linguistically isolated population above 20%”. In contrast to this Map, 
Colorado EnviroScreen does not itself define disproportionately impacted communities, 
except, as directed by HB 23-1233. While updating the mapping tools, CDPHE also 
updated the census data used for the Colorado Disproportionately Impacted Community 
Map from the American community survey (5 year) 2015-2019 dataset to the American 
community survey (5 year) 2018-2022 dataset. CDPHE is committed to continuous 
improvement of these mapping tools and will continue to update the Colorado 
Disproportionately Impacted Community Map to reflect the most recent data and meet 
the needs of the people and organizations it is intended to benefit. The use of an 
updated Disproportionately Impacted Community Map and its data in the future for 
purposes of Regulation Number 3 will not be required until the citations to the updated 
map within the regulation are revised and adopted through a rulemaking. Additionally, 
the Commission chose to include the statutory criteria for mobile home communities, 
which is in line with the revisions adopted to the definitions used in Regulation Number 
7 and accounts for the possibility of reportable emissions sources being located in 
mobile home communities. The Commission did not identify, target, or prioritize the 
criteria for Tribal lands provided for in § 24-4-109(2)(b)(II)(H), C.R.S., because the 
Commission’s permitting rules do not apply to areas under tribal jurisdiction, and this 
prong of the definition is therefore irrelevant to this rulemaking. Moreover, the 
Commission did not identify, target or prioritize the criteria for historically marginalized 
communities provided for in § 24-4-109(2)(b)(II)(E), C.R.S. because no party to the 
rulemaking presented evidence of historic discriminatory laws, policies, or practices, or 
evidence of present day environmental health disparities.  
The Commission adopted revisions in several sections to clarify the identification of 
disproportionately impacted communities in the permitting process by setting a date 
during the permitting process that solidifies the disproportionately impacted community 
status of a source for the remainder of the permitting process. The time between when 
permit application materials are submitted and the actual permit is issued can span 
months, during which time the disproportionately impacted community mapping tools 
and census data may be updated. Because there are regulatory requirements 
associated with a source being located in a disproportionately impacted community or 
cumulatively impacted community, there was confusion about which requirements a 
source that changes disproportionately impacted community status is subject to for the 
final permit. Therefore, the Commission adopted language in Part B, Sections III.B.4., 
III.D.2.c., III.D.2.d., and III.J.1.; and Part C, Sections III.C.15., V.C.5.d., and V.C.5.d.(i), 
establishing that a source is identified as being located in a disproportionately impacted 
community as of the date the permit application is received by the Division. The 
Commission clarifies that the permit application includes any materials submitted to the 
Division as part of a complete permit application under Part B, Section III.B. or Part C,

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
570 
Section III.C., including the associated environmental justice summary, ambient air 
quality analysis, and Air Pollutant Emission Notice (APEN), as applicable. The permit 
application process begins upon the date the earliest of the applicable materials is 
received by the Division, which is also the date used to assess the disproportionately 
impacted community status of the source. The disproportionately impacted community 
location status and associated requirements are in place for a source’s permit until the 
source submits materials initiating another permit application, at which time the 
disproportionately impacted community status would be reevaluated. 
The Commission also clarifies that the ambient air quality analysis refers to the 
materials submitted to the Division for purposes of assessing compliance with the 
National Ambient Air Quality Standards, pursuant to Part B, Sections III.B.7.d. and 
III.D.1.c. or Part C, Sections III.C.12.d. and IV.A., such as Form APCD-114. The 
Commission reiterates that the environmental justice summary is not always required, 
due to the exemptions in Part B, Section III.B.5.e. and Part C, Section III.C.14.a. The 
Commission further clarifies that the Division’s receipt of an initial permit application 
material does not trigger any timelines for determining completeness of an application. 
Pursuant to Part B, Section III.B.6. and Part C, Section IV.B., an application is not 
assessed for completeness until all information and data required to evaluate the 
application have been submitted to the Division. 
Additionally, the Commission adopted revisions to Part C, Section V.C.5.d.(i) clarifying 
the intention of the permitting requirement and restructuring the section to make it 
easier to understand, including breaking down the paragraph into multiple sub-
paragraphs. The purpose of the provision is for sources submitting operating permit 
renewals to review the emissions increases of any individual modifications of the 
operating permit since the last renewal to determine applicability of source-specific 
monitoring. For this provision, “the last renewal” may also refer to the initially issued 
operating permit. 
Further, the adopted revisions address typographical, cross-reference, and minor errors 
found within the regulation. 
To the extent that § 25-7-110.8, C.R.S., requirements apply to this rulemaking, and after 
considering all the information in the record, the Commission hereby makes the 
determination that: 
(I) 
These rules are based upon reasonably available, validated, reviewed, and 
sound scientific methodologies, and the Commission has considered all 
information submitted by interested parties. 
(II) 
Evidence in the record supports the finding that the rules shall result in a 
demonstrable reduction of VOCs and NOx emissions.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
571 
(III) 
Evidence in the record supports the finding that the rules shall bring about 
reductions in risks to human health and the environment that justify the costs to 
implement and comply with the rules. 
(IV) 
The rules are the most cost-effective to achieve the necessary and desired 
results, provide the regulated community flexibility, and achieve the necessary 
reduction in air pollution. 
(V) 
The rule will maximize the air quality benefits of regulation in the most cost-
effective manner.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-5 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
572
